evidence_quotes.quote: A common practice within the video game industry is to require connecting to an online server hosted by the publisher in order to be able to play the game.
Publishers implement and defend this practice by including language in their End User License Agreements (EULA) that purportedly gives them the right to remove access to the game at any time.

paragraphs.text: A common practice within the video game industry is to require connecting to an online server hosted by the publisher in order to be able to play the game. The reasons for this can be varied, but often include considerations such as Digital Rights Management / Anti-piracy, multiplayer connection and anti-cheat protections. Whilst these considerations can be valid, the online server connection requirement often becomes severely problematic for consumers once the publisher makes the decision to stop supporting the game and shut down the server. Once this happens, games are often rendered completely unplayable by those who have purchased it. A recent high profile example of this is Ubisoft's "The Crew", which is now no longer playable. Publishers implement and defend this practice by including language in their End User License Agreements (EULA) that purportedly gives them the right to remove access to the game at any time. Ubisoft's EULA Section 8: Termination states the following: "8. TERMINATION. The EULA is effective from the earlier of the date You purchase, download or use the Product, until terminated according to its terms. You and UBISOFT (or its licensors) may terminate this EULA, at any time, for any reason. Termination by UBISOFT will be effective upon (a) notice to You or (b) termination of Your UBISOFT Account (if any) or (c) at the time of UBISOFTs decision to discontinue offering and/or supporting the Product. This EULA will terminate automatically if You fail to comply with any of the terms and conditions of this EULA. Upon termination for any reason, You must immediately uninstall the Product and destroy all copies of the Product in Your possession." Not only does this state that Ubisoft may make the game unplayable at any time, they also demand that the consumer destroys a product in their possession - a product that they have purchased, no less. This practice is unfortunately quite common throughout the video game industry. It is also completely antithetical to any reasonable understanding of consumer rights and protections. Publishers should not be permitted to destroy or render purchased products inoperable at any time of their choosing. This practice should be banned under the Digital Fairness Act, and video game publishers should be required to ensure that games are left in a reasonably playable state after support ends. This is not a demand for perpetual support of games or products by the publishers - that would of course be unreasonable. The requirement should simply be that once support ends, an end of support / life plan is implemented by the publisher that allows consumers to keep their products in a reasonably functional state. This may mean that some aspects of a game change or are functionally degraded - for example, online matchmaking may need to be replaced by a local server hosting option. Such changes are reasonable for consumers to expect, and for developers and publishers to implement. That a game a consumer has purchased can arbitrarily be destroyed is not. An analogy: You purchase a game of Monopoly from your local bookstore. When purchasing, you are required to sign the game publisher's EULA. When you get home, you start a game with your family. Half-way through there is a knock on your door. Representatives from the publisher have arrived to reclaim the Monopoly board, as the company has decided to make use of the Termination clause in the EULA. You must give them back the board, and destroy the pieces and paper money that remain in your possession. You - naturally - protest that the game is yours, as you bought it from the shop. The representatives simply point to the EULA and threaten legal action if you do not comply. This scenario is of course unimaginable in the physical world. However, it is common practice in the digital world, and is exactly the sort of behaviour the Digital Fairness Act should prevent.
